top_profane.text:
As someone who has never knitted before in their life, I originally looked at this pin and thought, "What the hell is this world coming to." I mean, how much more crap can we haul in from Taiwan,anyway. There are landfills that should literally bear the label "Here We Have Crap From Taiwan", and again, "More Crap From Taiwan" and also, "Oh Look, It's Crap From Taiwan! Again." But then I thought of my Great Aunt Gertrude. She has been a knitter for a long time. One year, she knitted us boys matching sets of socks, mittens and underwear. Truly, she is our weirdest aunt.I'll never forget my father giving her a firm talking to on the front porch. He told her that no son of his would be wearing knitted panties, and she oughtta just stick to scarves and other outerwear. My brothers and I were peeking out the window,rootin' dad on. My aunt said nothing and handed dad his own pair of knitted man underpants.
For my high school graduation she didn't give money like my other relatives, but instead a pair of knitted boxers with John Lennon's face on them. In the you-know -what part. I will never forget the humiliation of opening that present at my grad party in front of all my football buddies.The day Lennon was killed is the only time I ever wore them. And I did so with great shame. I realized later (after using a urinal) that the only thing that could be worse than being killed was to be killed and then have some dude wear a semblance of your face on his short fronts.
Although she has brought me years of embarrassment and ugly useless gifts, I still wanted to give my aunt this pin. Well I brought it over one night, wrapped all nice. Aunt Dutie puts her glasses on, unwraps it, takes one look at it and says, "Why the HELL is that MOTH eating that Bill Cosby sweater?!?!" And the old bag threw it on the floor. Apparently the knitting needles and ball of yarn looked more like a moth to her.
She has since been committed to a home.
However, I showed the pin to my wife. I didn't tell her about my aunt's reaction.She immediately noticed a moth. My folks, too. They agreed, but my dad says the eyes make the moth look like Helen Hunt. Still a moth, but one that looks like Helen Hunt. I have brought this pin to the following places: my place of work, my peeknuckle club, the gas station, my church, restaurants that I frequent, my barber, my children,my grandchildren, and the local knitting shop. Everyone agrees. It looks like a moth. Though some people disagree with the Helen Hunt comparison and say that the moth looks more like Helen Mirren. Also many have said the moth favors Meryl Streep, which I can definitely see. The calm, quiet eyes certainly remind me of Streep the most. I haven't returned the pin. I don't know what the hell to do with it. I might stick it on the Christmas tree next year. Or maybe I'll give it to my other aunt, the one who collects taxidermy and gave me and my wife a stuffed flying squirrel for our wedding gift.
top_profane.title: Confusing.

I really like this bag. It’s beautiful and hip and I love how it will hold its structure and stand up (more like a breifcase) and not get all slouchy like a regular backpack. I didn’t notice a smell after the first couple days. Pockets and compartments are great. I wish the outer ones were slightly bigger to hold Mac power cord. It will fit but then getting it out is difficult. In the beginning 17” MacBook barely slid into one of the inside compartments but seems to be stretching out to fit nicely. Bag is well made and I am thinking I will be able to use it for years to come.

However, if you wear it as a backpack it’s pretty uncomfortable. I only walk a short distance in it but if you were in a city with a long commute it will dig into your back and that could get old. I only use as a backpack and not as messenger and it’s filled with super heavy crap so maybe that is contributing.

My other gripe is the buckles. I read the reviews about them but didn’t believe they could be that bad. They are. It’s extremely frustrating. You’ll just be walking and the buckle will just abruptly release and you have to grab the strap before your bag falls. I keep about 5k of computer crap in this thing so if it were to fall it would be traumatic. Sometimes I just get so annoyed I just carry it by the top handle (which is really sturdy) but defeats the point of this purchase. I have coffee and a toddler; I need hands free. I have tried to just redo the straps similar to the way the other reviewer posted but can’t get it to stay for more than a few days. I think I am just going to sew the straps so they can’t slide out of the buckles. That will mean going forward I can only wear it as a backpack but I think I can handle that commitment.

Still I love this bag and am too stubborn to quit using it even though I am annoyed on a daily basis.
top_profane.title: I have a Love Hate Relationship with this bag.

top_rant.text:
The two-sentence version of this review:
I bought this sleeve almost exactly 1 year ago for my 2017 15" MacBook Pro and it is still safely, beautifully, and perfectly protecting my laptop.

If you want thorough PROS, CONS, and OTHER THOUGHTS though, please read on.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

So, I'm the kind of consumer who:
1) loves details (so be prepared for a long review)
2) reads as many reviews as possible prior to purchase (ex. 5-6 hours of reading up on phone cases for my iPhone 7 plus)
3) will spend more for quality on something I'm going to constantly use 9 out of 10 times because I value stewarding what I am given well
Be prepared for all this.

PREFACE:
So, flashback to Sep 17, 2017, I was using a cheap Tomtoc sleeve to handle my new laptop—a 2017 15" Touch Bar MacBook Pro—while I was looking for a better option.
Which, on that note, don't get one if you've got this same laptop. It's useless as it's far too roomy, allowing the laptop to haphazardly shift around all the time. (https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B01H4ZRJ9I/ref=oh_aui_search_detailpage?ie=UTF8&psc=1)

Anyways, I had spent 10+ hours reviewing mass-produced cases on Amazon, pricey handmade Etsy ones, etc. and was starting to give up on my search. In my frustration, I decided I was going to be cheap and settle on a solid $40-50 case.
There was one brand that stood out to me and was constantly mentioned in reviews everywhere: Picaso Lab.

I did really love my my best bud's Picaso Lab sleeve that he'd had for years. Whenever I handled it it felt great to the touch and fit his MacBook Pro like a glove. After learning that I was temporarily using that cheap one, my friend rebuked and convinced me I too should get a Picaso Lab with one question:

You're not willing to spend $100 to protect your $2,400 laptop?

Bam. Right there. I pulled open Amazon Smile and bought mine (USE SMILE IF YOU AREN'T ALREADY! You'll be supporting charities and NGOs through your normal Amazon usage).

Now that I've used my Picaso Lab sleeve for a year, here are some comprehensive thoughts.
I'll start off with the short list of negatives and then segue into its wonderful positives.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

CONS:

1) EXPENSIVE
I'm not going to pretend it's not a lot of $; it is. This sleeve is a LUXURY. Also, a $2,400 laptop is a LUXURY. I could have spent that $100 and sponsored an underprivileged child's education for several months (World Vision - https://www.worldvision.org/sponsor-a-child). $2,400 would cover 1/6 the cost of providing clean, safe water and sanitation for a WHOLE community of hundreds if not thousands in Africa (The Water Project - https://thewaterproject.org/give-water).

So, PLEASE DON'T SPEND $ ON THIS if you think it could be of better use elsewhere for others. I encourage you to reconsider your financial decisions and be more selfless because most of us ought to.

However, I know my laptop and sleeve are going to be used to give back more than they cost thus I'd gladly make this purchase all over again. I am committed to serving and giving to those near and far because that's what Jesus would do and what we all should do. So, I know this sleeve is an investment to protect the effective tool that I use to bless others called a laptop.

2) IT'S A LITTLE LOOSE NOW
This might be me nitpicking, but I wish that my sleeve stayed as tight on my laptop as it did when I first received it.
If you look at Picture #3, you can see that there's a slight gap when my laptop is placed in my Picaso Lab. That gap wasn't there the first several months of use, but I've definitely put a ton of figurative and literal miles on this (more on that later in PRO point #1).

At first, I could put the MacBook Pro in the sleeve, hold it upside down, and vigorously shake it for 30 seconds and barely anything. All this would cause it to peak out just the tiniest bit. However, now I'll just hold it upside down and jiggle it side to side and it's what you see in Picture #2.

This isn't my ideal, but how often is someone sane going to hold an open-ended sleeve like that for a prolonged period of time? It's a bit of a bummer, but I'm grateful that this is such a negligible negative.

3) I WISH THERE WAS A CLASP OF SOME SORT

Yeah. I wish there was a strap thingamajig that kept the laptop from just-in-case falling out. See above con though and once again, negligible negative. Now onto all the things I really love about the Picaso Lab.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

THE SWEET PROS:

1) DURABILITY, DURABILITY, DURABILITY!
If there's a reason to buy a laptop sleeve or a $1,500 iPhone Xwhatever case or anything that is supposed to shield your pricey tech gear from the harsh, bitter world around it's this. I have no idea why anyone would buy something that looks great but does a crappy job at actually protecting their incredibly expensive investment. That's just vanity.
My black leather, red thread Picaso Lab sleeve is a BOSS though at keeping my MacBook Pro MINT.

After getting shoved into my backpack all the time, accidentally dropping it at short heights at least once (3-4 ft off carpeted ground), lugged around all over the West Coast then used in both hot and cold weather, and traveling with me halfway across the world and back this summer, my laptop has no visible or felt nicks or scratches. It is in actual perfect visible condition. It also serves as a good buffer between my laptop and any questionable surfaces underneath.

It's not a hard case, which would be the ultimate step-up for laptop protection, but the Picaso Lab is the next best thing. It has TOUGH EDGES AND CORNERS that have no give when I bump it on things and somehow simultaneously is SOFT to my laptop. It's probably just the natural, fantastic properties of good leather being good leather.

Granted, I am very careful with my laptop. I sometimes put it back in its sleeve when I'm going into a different room. However, I know the only thing that's ultimately keeping my MacBook Pro safe is my Picaso Lab sleeve. When it's in my backpack or I'm holding it under my arm, I am fully confident nothing is going to harm my laptop barring some freak accident like a biker barreling into me.

2) LOOKS (AND SMELLS) SMASHINGLY GREAT
I'm functionality over fashion almost every day, but there is no need to sacrifice when you own a Picaso Lab sleeve.
I still smile and admire the case at times because it's beautiful to both sight and touch.

The leather feels great and like any well-treated leather, it smells fantastic, too.
If you look at Picture #2, you'll see that the stitching is top-notch. Not one stitch is frayed despite the hundreds if not thousands of times I've handled it.

3) LIFETIME WARRANTY (OR FOR AS LONG AS ALEX IS ALIVE?)
I have no idea how old the Alex gentleman who makes these is, but for as long as he is alive he is a blessing to MacBook owners everywhere. Why? He has a LIFETIME WARRANTY attached to this stupendous product.

I don't know if he employs other workers or has passed/is passing his knowledge down to other skilled laborers, but if anything goes wrong with my sleeve, which will probably take a long time to happen or never transpire, I know I can contact Alex and get this taken care of. How am I confident I can get a hold of Alex for such a thing? See PRO #4.

4) HE IS A QUICK-RESPONDING REAL HUMAN BEING ON THE OTHER END
After admiring my sleeve, another buddy wanted a Picaso Lab but with black threading instead of red for an all-black leather sleeve. He texted Alex TODAY if he could make said alteration and Alex answered back to my friend in a text within an ACTUAL MINUTE. Alex was very helpful with every question and gave a super quick turnaround date.

Alex is the real stuff and you should also do business with him if you're wanting something custom. One more reason to buy a Picaso Lab, right? Now onto my final point and it only makes sense if you believe in the U.S.A..

5) SUPPORTING SKILLED, MADE IN THE U.S.A. LABOR
A worker is worth their wages and someone who does such a great job providing incredible products is definitely worth what they're selling. No matter where the product is coming from or what its creators background is, if something is excellent it is valuable and it deserves a price accordingly paid.
However, as a citizen of the United States of America, I believe in free enterprise that also invests into as much local economy as possible. Money has an exponential effect around us when its sown into the local economy. Financial advice aside, I myself am a Californian and when I read that Alex lives and makes these in California, it was a no-brainer cherry on top of my purchase.
I'm not being a patriot for patriotism's sake. I do believe though that the U.S.A, for all its faults, is the most privileged and free country in the world and provides the greatest opportunity for people to do good of any place in the world.
That might sound too preachy, but I want to put my money and support where my heart and mouth is and so this is just an added bonus to all the previously mentioned PROs.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

THOUGHTS:
All in all, invest the money to buy a Picaso Lab for yourself if you know that your laptop investment is worth making in the first place. I highly doubt you will regret your purchase and I am immeasurably grateful that I get to be the owner of such a hardy, quality, beautiful product backed by such great craftsmanship and a lifetime warranty. Buy yours today!
top_rant.title: 1-year later this is STILL 5 Stars

top_rant.text:
I was never someone who felt like stones were the way to go for me, but after the Pandemic I felt like I needed a cleanse of my inner life. I tried pills, thrills and cheap meals, but none of it worked. I bought these as a "why the hell not?" purchase and was not disappointed in the slightest! I found that after taking these stones I was able to change everything about my life! From the way I looked, talked and walked.
I want to go into a detailed review of each item purchased.
First was the Amethyst, since these were as small as they were, I chose to swallow this one whole. The star shape was rather intimidating, but by the time it made it past my throat, I developed a massive headache almost immediately. Trying Tylenol and other blood thinners, I found that the headache wouldn't go away, so I decided to try and sleep it off.
Once I was asleep I had this incredible dream about my father and I on a massive sea vessel. As the waves crashed against the ship I felt a throbbing on the top of my head. The more the waves crashed the sharper the pain. When I looked up, I saw my father floating above me with a hammer in one hand and an anvil in the other. Fire pouring from his mouth all over the top of my head. Once I noticed the fire was on me, it began to encompass my entire body, then began burning down the ship. The flames didn't scare me, but the waves of the ocean rose higher as more of the ship caught on fire. Eventually the boat was over taken by flames and the ocean waves. I reached out to my father yelling "Help me! Take my hand!" My father looked at the hammer in his hand and said with the fire still pouring from his mouth "I can't". Mad at my father, I screamed "put down the anvil then and save me." He looked at the anvil in his hand and said, with the fire raging "I can't". I yelled back, with the waves covering my mouth after every word, "Bring down your hammer and let me grab it, then hoist me up." The flames stopped pouring from his mouth and for the last time my father said, "I can't". With that, wings of fire came out of his back and he flew away. The water pulled me under and I woke up in a pool of sweat.
I was shocked! The dream had felt so real. I've always had a great relationship with my father, so this dream didn't coincide with the companionship we've built over the years.
I decided to take the next stone, in hopes for more answers. After the pain in my throat from the first, I chose to chew the next one. Chipping one of my molars.

The Lapis Lazuli didn't sit well in my stomach at first, so I did some online research about my dream to distract myself from the sudden stomach cramps. I found out some incredible truths about myself I would have never realized alone without the Lapis Lazuli. The sea vessel in my dream was me. I constantly see myself as a giant vessel of self-help and self-actualization, that I am the driver and the vehicle to my own desires, dreams and goals. I am the one holding up what I find most important, that the world is full of danger and fear, but only I can overcome those things with my abilities and gifts.
When I discover this truth, I'm in tears. I'm at my kitchen table, balling my eyes out with such possibilities, who and what could stop me.

By breakfast time I feel like it's a good time to take my next stone. I didn't see any instructions on if it was ok to take all 7 stones in one day, which is why I gave it 4 stars.

This time I take a hammer and smash up the next stone, which is the Blue Aventurine. I put the blue stone now turned to powder in my first cup of coffee in one of my biggest mugs. I honestly should have made the cup of coffee smaller, it was probably the most bitter thing I've ever tasted.
That was when I realized the meaning of the waves in my dream. They were the spoils of my work. The joy one experiences through going the extra mile, putting forth their best foot and reaping the benefits of a hard days work. That my desire for self improvement was vein and a false hope/desire that was pointless. This was a lot for me to take in at the time, it overwhelmed me that all I've stood for the past 17 years was nothing but mental self-satisfaction.

I stewed on the thought until lunch time.
I decided that it was time to take another stone.
The Green Aventurine ground up again and made a tea out of it, this time using a significantly smaller cup. I let the warm water sit on the stone for about 15 minutes and no change was made to the color of the water. No surprise there, this is stone we're talking about after all. But staring at the steam rising from the still water. I think of the pain on my head, my father and the fire pouring from his mouth. I keep hoping the color of the water will change as I stir up the stone and water as I take each sip, but the grains settle when I put the cup back down, making the water look like rippled glass. The pain in my dream being this pressure I put on myself, my father being that comfort I find in familiarity, the fire being change thrust upon me. I finish my Green Aventurine tea and decide to make another, using the Yellow Aventurine stone.

As I pour the new water in my cup, the memory of the pain on top of my head, from my dream disappears, was it a pressure pain? A burning sensation? I forget at the time. I just remember the hammer and anvil in my father's hands. My father is retired now, but he wasn't a blacksmith. He worked for an insurance company that took really good care of him and us when I was growing up. He made carrying the anvil look as light as the hammer, which those things are like 1,000 pounds. In my dream it just made sense that he was holding those things. He was always a strong man, he still is. I always remember him working hard, but leaving work at the front door and never bringing it home to the family.
I settle on my couch and stare out my window looking down at the shop across the street. It's a little sandwich shop, that hasn't adjusted well to the pandemic. They were shut down for over a year and reopened about a month ago. Someone had a dream about feeding someone their sandwiches and one day, outside of their control, a global disaster happens. I can't imagine how scared, how nervous, how excited they were, when they had the chance to reopen. Can you imagine? All their hopes and dreams came true, then put on an extended hold, just to have their dreams start anew.

The Red Carnelian I ground up and used as a seasoning for the asparagus I had as a side for dinner tonight. The fire destroying the ship as the waves overtake it and me crying for help. I'm scared. I'm nervous. I'm anxiety filled. I'm not finding the comfort I need in the places I want it. There's work to be done and no time to reflect. I have to move forward, to get uncomfortable. Occupy my mind with work, not plans! I don't want that. I don't want to go to the unknown. I don't think I can take it, but that's the point. How will I know how far I can go if I don't go out there and just make it happen.

I swallowed the RedJasper whole about an hour and a half ago. I'm off to sleep. No soreness in my throat this time. No pain in my head. I'm excited to dream tonight. I'm excited about the work that will be done tomorrow by my hand. Work that I can look back on and be proud of. Work that may not matter to anyone, but it will matter to me, because it will be done by my head and my hands.

Great product though, would definitely buy again!
top_rant.title: Great stones!

This is a pearl necklace to where you get what you pay for. Pearls don't quite match, aren't quite round, and don't have much luster. The clasp is pot metal. That said, construction is OK. There's a knot between each pearl, so it's put together well. For the price, this is a situation where you get what you pay for. It's definitely for more of a casual or bohemian look, or good for small girls where you want to gift something real but not super high end.

Are the pearls real?: First off, Jacqueline Kennedy's iconic pearl necklace was fake. It was painted glass. When it went to auction in the 1990s it was appraised at four hundred dollars, just as jewelry and not with any Kennedy connection (of course it sold for more). That was a while ago, there's been inflation, and that's already more than ten times what this Crane Pearl necklace costs even before inflation. My point is that in this price range, fake and real overlap. For a necklace, unless you are spending four figures or up, fake pearls can be the better quality over real pearls. In this price range, what you see is what you get and looks is all that matters. There's a world of difference between low grade plastic pearls and high quality glass with several layer of paint to get a good luster. Now that that's out of the way, this necklace is real pearls. They aren't terrible, but they aren't great quality. Shapes are semiround and are irregular, and it shows from across a room. Shapes and sizes don't match across the necklace, and the size variation is enough that it shows from across a room. They don't have a ton of luster.

The look: So... these pearls are not graded very well. But also, this necklace is thirty dollars. To me, you get what you pay for. This isn't terrible. It won't have that classic pearl necklace look. The product pic of the woman in a business suit wearing these is a total lie. These are too irregular, and it's better to get a higher grade of matched pearls in the hundred dollar price range if you want to look corporate or polished. Instead, this Crane Pearl necklace has more of a beachy bohemian vibe. The size and shape makes the necklace look a little bumpy and chunky from across the room. Meanwhile, pearls have that beachy vibe. They come from the ocean, and they are white rather than bright but have an interesting sheen. This necklace can be a great casual look. This also can be a good gift for small girls, because these are smaller pearls with a fresh innocent look, and the irregular shapes can work for children who maybe aren't as dressy because they play rough even when dressed up. Also, it's a good gift for smaller girls, because they are real, and so getting into nicer things, but casual and low cost enough that the child can use them. I would say, if this is for you, then plan on wearing it with a bohemian sundress or peasant top look. And it's also a good "first jewelry" gift for girls who are old enough to operate the clasp.

Length: This is adjustable from 16 to 18 inches. That's a classic length for a pearl necklace. The biggest length is still to where someone has to operate the clasp behind their neck. It's a lobster claw style clasp, which is a little harder to operate. That's a limitation.

Construction: The clasp to this is pot metal. It looks like something that will resist corrosion. Some other reviews say it's sterling silver, and it most definitely is not a silver. The strand of pearls is on thick thread that is knotted between each pearl, which is great. That keeps the pearls from scratching against each other and gives a nice even spacing. It also means that if the string breaks, you loose only one pearl, and can take the rest home and restring it with a new silk thread. Nothing at all is wrong with how this is constructed. It's every bit as well done as a much more expensive pearl necklace. (The low price is the irregular pearls, but they did the pearls right and quality of the settings and stringing is fine.)

For gifting: I wouldn't gift this to an adult, unless you got it for you and a friend truly seems to admire it. I do think it's great for girls, where you want to gift something real, but you also want to ensure the child is responsible with their things before gifting something super expensive. The way this is put together is nice with the knots between each pearl, and it is real so has some heft to it. The biggest limitation for gifting is that the lobster claw clasp is a style of clasp that may be difficult for some to operate, and this is short enough that it has to be clasped and unclasped to take it off and on and can't be fit over the head. I would say, it's a good gift for ages 8 and up girls, but even as an adult, I tend to avoid lobster claw clasps.

Overall, this is fine for what it is. It's a classic length strand of irregularly shaped and sized pearls. The look is not classic sleek business. Instead, the slight irregularities show from a distance. This has a beachy bohemian vibe, and is better for casual looks. This is a case of you get what you pay for, and if you want the sleek classic look, I think you are looking at hundred dollars and up on a different necklace. That said, this is real pearls, and the way they are strung and presented is perfect, with knots between each pearl to prevent them scratching. It can be a great gift to a child, provided the child is old enough to operate the clasp, because it is real adult jewelry so has some quality to it, but also isn't terribly expensive to where someone might protect it from children. It's short enough that it needs to be clasped and unclasped to take off and on, so if gifting, try to check whether the recipient likes and wears necklaces with this style of clasp.
top_rant.title: Irregular shaped pearls give a beachy bohemian vibe; well constructed with knots between pearls

So far with my kids, I have kept them out of air conditioned areas, and just had them wearing jersey knit 100% cotton face masks that aren't particularly tailored. With school being in person or bust this coming year, I am getting additional face masks so that I will always have enough as the kids loose them. This one seems good for young kids maybe toddler to early elementary. It seems to be all cotton as described, and the fabric seems good enough. It doesn't have nose wire, which is the only flaw (assuming you get the fit right for your child's age).

Size: I have 4 kids, ages 1, 3, 5, and 7. These masks are a perfect fit for the 3 year old, and also fit the 5 year old. The 5 year old seems to have a small face. These are a bit small for the 7 year old. He is almost 8. They work for him, but for that age I would go a little scootch bigger. For immediate use for a 7 year old, they are OK, but I'm kind of assuming things will last long enough for masks to be outgrown. The 1 year old pretty much takes anything off, including regular clothes, but these masks seem like they would be a good for for her.

Material and filtering: These are 3 layers of cotton - 2 of good quilting cotton and 1 of a more open weave. The outside pattern is two pieces of patterned quilting cotton put back to back and sewn together. Then inside there is a layer of white cotton fabric that makes a pocket to where you can slide a disposable filter between the colored front pieces and the white inner piece. The 5 pack of masks came with some disposable filters. I feel that the cotton is good enough quality to work well as a filter. I had read about filtering and 100% cotton in a quilting weight does pretty well as a filter. The 2 layers of quilting cotton that make the colored front part of the mask seem good.

Fit: These do not have any wire in the nose. I have found that for me, a gap at the bridge of my nose is the biggest issue with mask fit. For kids, I see more masks that aren't particularly tailored, and something is better than nothing, but a nose wire would allow for a better fit to keep air from going around the edges of the mask. Other than that they are good. The ear loops are adjustable with a silicone bead that slides to make them tighter or looser. I tied a knot in each earloop on the outside past the silicone bead, to make it so my kids can't pull the beads off. Even with the knot, there was enough loop to be able to adjust and get a good fit on each.

Style: The stars is a good gender neutral option, where it has some pattern so you can recognize it and kids can know which mask is theirs. Meanwhile, the boys are fine with it and the girl is fine with it. The boys are at an age where they are whiny about not wearing girl stuff. Girl wants to be super girly, but says she likes these OK, I guess because it's a neutral. Anyway, the stars are a pattern but it's so tame that it's basically just a pattern for kids to see and recognize their mask, but seemingly not enough of a pattern to offend any of them. That seems good for kids. I got the "Multi #4" pack. In person, the red is a little more vivid, and the other colors look about the same compared to the online pics. The overall color tone is fairly neutral with even the green being dark enough to be neutral with the red as the only real color in the pack.

Problems: There is no nose wire. So there almost certainly will be a gap around the nose where air will go around the mask rather than through it. The silicone beads can be pulled off. Kids will pull them off. Like I said, I tied a knot in each earloop out past the silicone bead and that keeps the beads on. When I did that, there was still enough earloop to get a comfortable fit for the kids. I put the knot out at the edge, so when you tie the knot make it out as far as you can so you don't use up the loop. If your kids do pull the bead off, you can take dental floss, thread it through the ear loop, then put both ends of the dental floss through the silicone bead and use it to pull the earloop back through the bead. So, it's a fixable problem, but fix it right when you get these and before your kids pull the beads off and loose them.

Overall, these are an OK basic mask for kids. These masks fit the toddler to young elementary age, but I think for older elementary, maybe these are a little small. Masks seem to be 100% cotton and just a bit tailored. That's a positive. The only flaw is that it doesn't have nose wire, so some air will leak at the nose. Compared to bulk packs of stretchy fabric masks from relatives, Costco, and Amazon, these are toward the high end for quality. That's because sometimes the internet says "100% cotton" but it's really polyester. These masks are what they say there are, and 5 masks in a pack is enough to have some spares. If you find masks elsewhere with nose wire and good material, then go for the nose wire for a better fit.
top_rant.title: Good cotton and adjustable earloops make these an ok basic for toddler to early elementary

top_rant.text:
I now have this product in 3 colors, and I have 2 more bags of the Vanilla on the way. I have a small family run plant boutique where most of our products are shipped via USPS Priority mail, but I value the safety of the plants, the environment, and my clients, so instead of using stuffing that is not biodegradable, I have traditionally used the crinkle-cut kraft paper because it does an OK job of mimicking shredded newspaper but prettier. I am not selling cheap plants, nor am I selling mass quantities of plants forcing me to need the cheapest packaging supplies available, so I have the luxury to pass on a little luxury to my clients.

In the photo I have attached, you see a bag of the crinkle-cut craft paper on the left--a 1 lbs bag that is not compressed, so the volume does not increase much when you remove it from the plastic bag. On the right, you see THIS product, the shredded tissue paper, after it has been removed from the compressed paper packaging (think the size and thickness of a pair of ladies pantyhose or tights) then fluffed up to show the actual volume of the product when not compressed. It goes about as far as the crinkle-cut craft paper, and both will re-compress inside a package to be mailed, so honestly 1 package is not a lot of either product for my purpose--but for anyone using this for filling gift boxes, it's PERFECT (and I really love it for my purpose as well, albeit not the most cost efficient).

The reason I am writing this review as well as including the photo above is because I not only feel it IS a great product as described, but there were just so many people on one of the other colors leaving negative reviews based on the size of the product. Some had not even opened the package--others had removed it form the package then didn't fluff it to reconstitute the proper size. These negative reviews claim the product to be a "rip off" and also make claims about how it isn't enough to fill 1 gift box, let alone 3 which they needed it for.... YES IT IS. These negative reviewers apparently neve read the package which is marked clearly above the cellophane window showing the color swatch: "COMPRESSED in airtight packaging for eco reasons, take me out and give me a good fluff up to regain my volume!" Well, I did read it, and I followed the instructions, and I ended up with more volume than the 1 lbs of crinkle-cut craft paper. This product is much softer and more gentle for these delicate plant leaves than craft paper. The same volume of a sheet of tissue paper will weigh dramatically less than a sheet of craft paper of equal size or "volume" which is what matters here---I went so far as to compare matching the volume of a jar of nails to a jar of cotton balls which are the same size, but if you try to match a pound of nails to a pound of cotton, naturally you will have far more cotton than nails... this is the same thing, just with a heavy, harsh paper next to a lightweight, delicate paper. For my needs and generally my visual and tactical preferences, I like the shredded tissue MUCH better than the shredded craft paper, or shredded newspaper!

Cost efficiency: the same volume of the cut craft paper cost me $17.55 after CA sales tax, while the shredded tissue paper cost 6.95 pre-tax, and $7.66 after CA sales tax, so I paid around $15 for 2 more packages of Vanilla which will more than double the volume of the cut craft paper, but still be more than $2 cheaper.

**Note worth mentioning: someone in one of the pink colors, I think a rose color, said that Target sells a "similar product" for approx $2. I have not had an opportunity to check this out, but if it's true I'll obviously have to switch to save money, but I am suspicious of this price and the term "similar product" because it is so easy to think something looks the same on the rack, but we cannot tell if it really is the same until we get it home and do a true side-by-side comparison as I have done with my former fave, the crinkle cut stuff vs. this awesome new shredded tissue paper.

Eventually, I'll need to just buy a large shredder and shred my own HAHA but for now, I really like and appreciate this product for what it is, and I especially appreciate how they have taken the time to choose packaging designed to be eco-friendly as opposed to the plastic ziploc sack my old product comes in. I don't work with plants because I don't care about the environment, after all.... my love for nature and our planet led me to my love for plants, which led to the business, which would be very counterintuitive IMO if I were to then NOT do all I can to protect unnecessary waste and plastics as much as possible.

HTH someone!
top_rant.title: So much nicer than crinkle-cut craft paper!

top_rant.text:
This press is incredible. It’s just wonderful. Success from the very first go.

I had initially purchased a Victoria cast iron press after having read reviews about how they were the “Rolls Royce” of presses. Then, before using it, and having had a chance to put it together and take a look at it, I read all the further reviews about how cast iron presses crack, the handles shear, etc; just from normal use. Also, folks complained that they just couldn’t get their tortillas, roti, etc thin enough and we’re still needing to roll them further with a pin to get them to the desired shape. Waaat? So I began to question the wisdom of my purchase. It became quickly apparent that I had made the wrong choice for us.

We decided that we would return the cast iron press; unused, and invest in the press we truly felt would deliver the results we were looking for, and have a tool that accomplished our goals from the outset.

My husband and I made this our Mother’s Day / Father’s Day gifts to each other for this year. (I’d get the press and he’d receive hand made tortilla, roti, flat breads etc. 😉)

I admit, this purchase was an investment for our budget, but I will say this press has been nothing but a joy. A joy to admire, and a joy to use, a joy to own. I made 6-8” corn tortillas on my first go, and cooked them on my little (10” Lodge) cast iron griddle. They turned out beautifully and I knew we’d made a wise decision.

If you want to invest in an item that will be a pleasure to own, serve you for a lifetime, and deliver great (thin pressed) results from the get go ... this is the press to buy. I did opt for the largest size to give myself some wiggle room and just to be as flexible size-wise as possible. You can always make smaller items in a larger press, but you can’t make larger items in a smaller press! ☺️

In this day of mass produced semi disposable products I love the fact that our purchase supported a California Artisain and his wife and that pleases me greatly. This press will no doubt serve us well for the remainder of our lives, and then be passed on to our daughter (though she may own her own by then) or a grandchild. In any case this is a momorable and favored amazon purchase that has been nothing from a genuine joy ... right out of the box.

I recommend this press, or one similar, as there are several models to choose from to anyone looking to purchase a quality item that will deliver time after time. I honestly can’t think of any way it could be improved upon. It’s just *that* nice.

**UPDATE** End of July 2018

I wanted to leave a little update to say that we continue to use this press frequently and L❤️VE it, every time. It’s easily within our top five favorite amazon purchases of all time and we will be grateful for having found such an outstanding product for the remainder of our lives.

I’d like to share just a few tips that I’ve picked up along the way. We do add a touch of either lard or even butter to our masa and easily press out up to 10” wafer thin corn tortillas with ease. Seriously. No rollig, nothing. I’ve tried different materials inside to line the press; parchment, ziplock bag material, and clean recyclable grocery bags cut to aprox 11 x 11” squares. By FAR, my favorite material to use is the inelegant disposable grocery store shopping bags cut to squares. It’s thin enough that the masa just spreads out but not so thick that the fragile, thin tortillas tear when being removed from the plastic as they did with the (commonly recommended) ziplock plastic. I wash the plastic pieces afterward and dry them between two dish towels and then store it with the press so it’s handy next time we need it. (Truly recycled!) It took some experimenting to find the best method for me so I wanted to share this with others in hopes of making your journey a bit easier.

After pressing, I just put the tortilla in my hand and gently peel off the pliable plastic, and flip I over into the pre heated cast iron comal / griddle ... also purchased on amazon (10” by Lodge; very economical and JUST the right size!)

Anyway. It’s a lot of money, but it supports a self sustaining artisain, (which I really like) AND it makes the best tortillas you’ll ever have ... for the rest of your life. It’s even beautiful to look at.

Couldn’t be happier with this purchase. 😊

top_rant.text:
I knit a lot, and I've bought dozens of decorative stitch marker sets over the last couple of decades. Good stitch markers can keep me plowing through challenging or frustrating projects because the joy of seeing them pass through my hands offsets or outweighs the irritation a pattern can cause. Bad stitch markers can send even stockinette TV knitting into hibernation. These are good stitch markers.

The main ring, the part the needles pass through and the yarn is most affected by, appears entirely seamless. These aren't a spiral wire cut into split rings but genuine metal rings.

The single negative I've found about them -- and it's specific to the Small size for size 7 needles and under -- is that for fingering/sock-weight yarn that's not being used in lace, the rings are thick enough I have to pull a little extra on each stitch after a marker to avoid loose-stitch ladders caused by the extra distance the yarn covers to span the thick rings. The effect is almost negligible, but it's more pronounced than (for example) the loose purl stitch caused by a yarn-forward (not yarn over) coming from knit to purl in ribbing. Where the stitch marker falls between knit and purl in ribbing, it amplifies that effect noticeably. These aren't appropriate markers for doing lots of sock-weight ribbing, so I'll have to use other markers for socks. That's a minor inconvenience to me since, as I mentioned, I have dozens of stitch-marker sets and hundreds of little plain, thin ones. The kind of stitch marker that uses a loop of thin, flexible wire is still my favorite for not creating a gap.

That said, this is a very minor and specific way in which these stitch markers aren't the most suitable kind for a particular task, and if I were on vacation with nothing but sock yarn and these markers, I'd use them happily anyway.

The trade-off is that these can be used with perfect ease for other types of knitting that most stitch markers are bad to awful for. Lace knitting with lace-weight yarn is one of the most notoriously picky kinds, as ready to snag on even the nicest join of a split ring as on the exposed wire-ends of a wire loop. Any yarn that's even a little fuzzy or that splits easily will snag on most types. Smooth or slick laceweight or cobweb-weight yarns love to slip through mediocre joins and end up passing through a split ring instead of around it, trapping the marker in place. The cat-bead forms the bottom of these markers, and the ring is smooth and solid -- nothing to snag at top or bottom. There is only one exposed wire-end, since the top end goes back down into the top bead. The lower end of the wire is pressed against the lower bead under a little bit of tension from the wire at the top. This isn't a unique or new construction, but it's a tried-and-true construction that's particularly well-executed here. I will happily confirm the seller's claim that this is "Knitting jewelry that is snag free."

At one point, I started making my own stitch markers, trying to save money. However, making stitch markers (or other types of jewelry) is a skill in its own right that requires practice, patience, and materials -- time and money I decided I'd rather send knitting than spend getting good enough to make better markers than the mediocre ones available for sale. In other words, I gave up trying to make ideal stitch markers because I wasn't willing to go to the lengths required to make ones as good as these.

That last sentence is the answer to any questions about whether the price is fair. Yes, they probably cost the maker only a few dollars for materials -- but maybe a few hundred for lighting, magnification, special pliers, a workspace, and other tools to produce pretty, uniform, and functional ones instead of stabby, staggering, misshapen ones. And that doesn't get into the time spent gaining the experience to produce them reliably and promptly, on-demand. I can make bad stitch markers for $5. I can buy mediocre ones for $8-10 a set elsewhere. Since I don't want to fiddle with bad or mediocre stitch markers instead of spending that time knitting, I'll happily pay $15-20 for these. It's fair pricing for the quality.

Summary: Speaking as someone who's been knitting for 40 years and who cares more than the average knitter about the markers I use: Not only would I buy these again, I have a second set in my shopping cart already. I will be delighted to buy others for future projects. I give them my highest recommendation for everything except knitting socks, for which better ones could be found but for which these are still good.

top_rant.text: I live in Maine and maybe our squirrels and birds are larger.  The feeder itself is awesome and the owner put his heart and soul into it, it is so obvious.  It is the Best Feeder Hands down.  The problem comes in with the holes on both sides.  The holes are not big enough.  I suppose it would be for the red smaller squirrels, but not the gray ones here in Maine.  All the animals share the feeders at my place, it is a matter of who gets their first and their size.  So the smaller ones feed later in the afternoon, after the larger ones have their fill in the morning and there is no stopping the Blue Jays from getting the peanuts.  They love them.  I have tried hiding the peanuts under seed and the Jays find them and take one, and two if they really try hard.  I love all the animals outside no matter, skunks can find their own food.  It wouldn't be so bad if they didn't get spooked and leave a stench.  Anyway this is about the Birds.  I noticed someone said that even the birds eat out of this feeder.  They sure do and one Freezing morning, I checked out the door and said Gee there is plenty of food.  It was dark in the feeder, I thought it was food.  That afternoon, I checked again and I saw something struggling in the jar.  I thought is that a squirrel?  I went to look closer and it was a Mourning Dove that had gone into feeder jar and then couldn't get back out.  Her body was too long.  I felt horrified.  I tried to calm her down.  I talked gently to her in the jar and tried to angle the jar and her body weight and length to better allow her to stick her head out and then understand that she needed to move her whole body in that direction to get out.  She maneuvered to do that fluttering frantically to somehow get the process done and over with.  I wondered if she was alright or would have a heart attack before getting out, but she managed to do what I had hoped she would and that was to point toward the glass opening and see that was how to get free.  When out, I was going to check her all over to see if she had broken a wing or anything, but she flew off so I knew she was fine.  She flew to her usual tree for the rest of the night to rest.  The next morning I saw her with a bunch of her friends by the feeder all eating happily.  I had taken the glass jar off the feeder and decided the feeder would be hers and leave the glass off.  The food would stay dry and not frozen like in a planter I have one of the feeding troths for the squirrels.  She was fine and she was grateful too.  She left me a pine cone.  I knew she realized I wasn't hurting her but trying to help her and I realized too that she understood that the feeder was now hers and all of her friends with special food just for those types of birds.  They really don't eat sunflower seeds.  Anyway, it turned out well.  It was quite a scare though.  I could now feel better about the feeder and still use it for her and her friends.  So I'm happy with the feeder and I have an extra jar for the squirrels, but the feeder need much larger holes on the sides and the height of the wooden portion should be taller to accommodate squirrels sitting up in the feeder which is especially helpful during inclement weather.  It helps them stay warm instead of being cold after being wet.  I worried about that too.  So it is an excellent quality feeder, best wood and awesome job, but needs to be proportioned a little differently to accomodate all sizes of animals.  I only took one star off because of the scare both her and i got and if no one had of rescued her, she would have died for sure.  The feeder is Awesome and I didn't loose out any of the use of the feeder or jar.  But the scare was a knock of one star.  I do appreciate the feeder a lot, so I wonder if the squirrels and birds are bigger at my place.  They could very well be because of the environment.  There is plenty of food except during winter and they really should be hibernating at this time, but some don't fully and do get up and about, but they are usually not fully awake, something just stumbled them out of their sleep.  So take these things into consideration so as not to be disappointed.  The size could be related to the area.  Hope this was helpful.

I bought this sticker for my workout space. I love it, but wanted to write so people would understand the size which is very unclear on the site. This is the 35 X 50 size in DARK GRAY and is on an 8 foot wall. It is much larger than I expected, but I have grown to love it. I have reviewed the "Achieve" sticker from this company as well that I have on the opposite wall under another review. As you can see, I didn't install it as it was laid out. I liked the spacing that another customer did and I did something similar. This sticker is Dark Gray and I love it on my green/gray walls. I read tons of reviews about how to install it and I will say that you MUST put a single line of blue tape down the center of each section of the sticker at least 8 inches above the top of the sticker and 8 inches below the sticker so it holds it securely, then work only on all of the letters from one side first. KEEP THE CENTER LINE IN PLACE AFTER THE FIRST SIDE IS COMPLETE. You must use a level. For my wall as a reference, I made the "N" of NO my center of the wall and worked from there. The sticker comes in several parts but I had to cut it to get this layout, just be sure you are cutting to allow enough room for the sticker under and above each message so it will adhere to the wall(In other words, don't cut it too close to the letter, leave an inch or so above and below). It really takes two people to put it up and it took us about a half an hour to complete this layout versus 2 hours for the "Achieve" sticker. This sticker has letters that are all one piece versus the "Achieve" which has many pieces for each letter and is quite the process adhering it to the wall. My wall for reference had just been painted a week prior over orange peel. After you have leveled the message, which you will be able to see on the wall and TAPED A LONG PIECE DOWN THE CENTER OF EACH SECTION then one person will peel back the adhesive very slowly as CLOSE TO THE WALL AS POSSIBLE while the other person follows closely as the transfer comes off of the transfer paper onto the wall with a credit card (they offer a tool to purchase but a credit card is perfect) to SLOWLY make sure each letter comes off of the transfer paper and onto the wall. The letters will absolutely tear if you don't go slowly and thoughtfully. After you each letter, we skimmed over the letter with the card again to make sure it had adhered then onto the next letter. After that half was done, we smoothed the entire letters AGAIN with the credit card and it adhered perfectly. When all letters of that half are secure and ONLY THEN you can remove the long center tape and then do the process to the other side. It is imperative that the person who is pulling off the adhesive do it slowly and as close to the wall as possible so the letters transfer. My room is what they call a "bonus" room in California, it is 22 feet long and 13 feet wide and I have a reformer, TV, couch, trampoline, boxing bag, weights and a yoga swing in it. It has saved me during this pandemic as I used to be a gym rat and now I can do everything at home. I looked for art for weeks and finally settled on this sticker and I couldn't be happier. I will say that my husband thinks this looks "gaudy" so you need to decide for yourself, but it is MY SPACE and I love it. I work out every day and when I am feeling exhausted I look to this wall and especially to my "Achieve" wall and find a word to focus on and keep going. I love it and hope I have helped you with your decision about placement and size. Just remember that this is the 35 X 50 size and I can't imagine going bigger unless you have very tall walls and high ceilings! Happy working out!

I decided to add the other sticker wall with the 60 X 30 "Achieve" sticker for reference so you could see sizing but this main information is for the "Will it BE EASY" sticker. I would have gone bigger with the "Achieve" sticker had I bought them at the same time.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I purchased Gardenia because I really like that scent. It is a scent that does not create an allergy with me or bother my nose or throat. This is natural oils, and so far the fragrance is pungent enough for me. I have a small apartment 550sf so several sprays in each room is enough.

The real test on that though is to leave your home or apartment for several hours and see if you can still smell the fragrance when you return. I can. Also I can smell it lightly later in the evening after spraying in the morning. So that's very good in my book.

It has taken care of my musty air problem and it also has taken care of my trash/garbage odor problem in this small apartment so I don't have to take it out so often. Both cans have a lid, but at times I used to smell the trash - this fragrance has taken care of that problem.

I also use it in my linen closet and clothes closet which is very nice.

Also, the spray nozzle is very good. Does not build up in hole and does not drip or leak (so far) from the sprayer or the turn-cap, which is very important because you have to shake the contents before spraying. The spray is a short-burst spray about 10"-12" wide and about 6"-8" out/up and down. So it is adequate for me in my apartment. I have to do 2 or 3 sprays in each area. But that is fine for me. I purchased the 8 oz size and I believe it is going to last me a good while. The contents is not under pressure and is in a dark blue plastic container. I cannot tell how far down the plastic tube goes in the bottle. Hopefully it goes down pretty far so that not much is wasted when the contents gets down to the bottom. Have not tried it in my car yet.

As an aside, I would like the ingredients to be listed on the label - as best can be done for the consumer without infringing on owner proprietary patent ingredients. It just should be restated, as it is on Amazon, the general ingredients and that they are safe and non-toxic for humans, pets, and the environment.

Also on the label should be the website contact, customer svc. number, home-base information of KOKU. i.e. if I had a physical business where clients visited and I had a restroom for them and put this in the restroom, they would certainly want to know where to obtain KOKU, because the fragrance is lovely. Amazon is a great place, but would they assume to look on Amazon first?

Thank you for an excellent product.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Lovely Scent  - Great Product

top_five_star_obscene.text: I recently turned 60 and am watching, horrified, as our precious planet slowly (and sometimes quickly) disintegrates.  There is no one generation to blame (I grew up washing aluminum foil and plastic bags, taking my lunch to school in a brown paper bag that had to be used until it fell apart, and not knowing what a disposable cup was until I saw friends’ moms buying them); all generations are in some part guilty.  But every generation, every single person, can begin to make a difference TODAY, and this wonderful bar is a great example of the way.  Not only is there no plastic involved, it WORKS!  This is the first conditioner bar I’ve tried (having used bar shampoo ever since my first trip to Europe in 2004) and it works so well I won’t try another.  I love it for my skin, too!!!  My legs are very soft and look healthy even three days after using this in the shower.  My hair stays cleaner longer, too, compared to crap in a plastic bottle.  Try this bar.  Oh, and it lasts a very long time; I’ve had mine for two months and it looks like the day I bought it, but I don’t shower every day.  Get the smaller size for travel.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Unbelievably good product at a great price.  Buy the smaller bar for travel (-1 ⭐️ explained).
